---
title: "Background Primer"
subtitle: "Everything you need before the lesson — and nothing you don't"
---

This primer covers the four ideas the [Bayesian statistics lesson](index.qmd)
assumes. If you can comfortably answer the self-check at the bottom of each
section, you're ready. Total reading time: about 30 minutes.

## 1. Probability as a measure of belief

A probability is a number between 0 and 1 that measures how strongly you
expect something. 0 means "impossible," 1 means "certain," and 0.5 means
"could go either way."

Two mental models coexist, and both are useful:

- **Long-run frequency:** "This coin lands heads with probability 0.5" means
  that across many flips, about half come up heads.
- **Degree of belief:** "I'm 80% sure I locked the door" is a perfectly
  meaningful probability even though there's no repeatable experiment — the
  door is either locked or it isn't. The 80% describes *your uncertainty*,
  not the door.

Bayesian statistics leans on the second reading. That's what makes it
applicable to one-off questions — *will this candidate win?*, *does this
patient have the disease?* — where "repeat the experiment many times" makes
no literal sense.

::: {.callout-note}
## Self-check 1
The weather app says 30% chance of rain tomorrow. Tomorrow it rains. Was the
app wrong? *(Answer: not necessarily — a single outcome can't invalidate a
probability. What would discredit the app is if, across many "30%" days, it
rained far more or less often than 30% of the time. That property is called
**calibration**, and it reappears in Module 4 of the lesson.)*
:::

## 2. Joint and conditional probability

**Joint probability** is the chance of two things both being true:
$P(A \text{ and } B)$. Example: the probability that a randomly chosen person
is left-handed *and* plays guitar.

**Conditional probability** is the chance of one thing *given* that another is
true, written $P(A \mid B)$ — "the probability of A given B." The vertical bar
is pronounced "given," and everything to its right is treated as an
established fact.

The two are connected by the definition that all of Bayesian statistics is
built on:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}$$

In words: among the situations where B happens, what fraction also have A?

**A concrete example.** In a class of 100 students, 40 drink coffee, and 10
students both drink coffee and are morning people. Then

$$P(\text{morning person} \mid \text{coffee}) = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25.$$

Note what happened: conditioning on "drinks coffee" *shrank the world* from
100 students to 40, and we recounted inside the smaller world. That's all
conditioning is — shrinking the world and recounting.

::: {.callout-note}
## Self-check 2
Same class: 50 students are morning people. What is
$P(\text{coffee} \mid \text{morning person})$? *(Answer: 10/50 = 0.20 —
different from the 0.25 above. That asymmetry is the subject of the next
section.)*
:::

## 3. The direction of the bar matters — a lot

$P(A \mid B)$ and $P(B \mid A)$ are different numbers, sometimes wildly
different. This sounds obvious stated abstractly, yet confusing them is
probably the most consequential statistical error humans make:

- $P(\text{positive test} \mid \text{healthy})$ might be 5%, while
  $P(\text{healthy} \mid \text{positive test})$ can be 84% — same test, same
  population. Doctors and patients routinely conflate the two.
- In court: $P(\text{evidence} \mid \text{innocent})$ being tiny does **not**
  mean $P(\text{innocent} \mid \text{evidence})$ is tiny. This confusion is
  called the **prosecutor's fallacy**, and it has produced real wrongful
  convictions — most infamously the 1999 UK case of Sally Clark, convicted
  after a medical expert squared a 1-in-8,543 probability to get "1 in 73
  million," neglecting both the dependence between events and the base rate
  of the alternative explanation. The conviction was overturned in 2003.

An extreme example makes the asymmetry vivid:

> $P(\text{dead} \mid \text{struck by lightning})$ is high.
> $P(\text{struck by lightning} \mid \text{dead})$ is minuscule.

Bayes' theorem is exactly the tool that converts one direction of the bar
into the other — legitimately, using the base rate. Which brings us to the
last prerequisite.

::: {.callout-note}
## Self-check 3
About 90% of professional basketball players are over six feet tall. Does it
follow that 90% of people over six feet tall are professional basketball
players? Why not — what number is missing? *(Answer: no; you need the **base
rates** — there are millions of tall people and only a few hundred NBA
players, so $P(\text{NBA} \mid \text{tall})$ is close to zero.)*
:::

## 4. Base rates

The **base rate** is how common something is in the relevant population
before you look at any specific evidence: 1% of patients have the disease,
0.1% of passengers are carrying a prohibited item, 45% of emails are spam.

Base rates are the "prior" in Bayesian language, and the reliable finding
from decades of psychology research (Kahneman & Tversky's work on
representativeness in the 1970s and onward) is that **humans neglect them**
when given vivid specific evidence. Told that Steve is "shy and tidy," people
judge him more likely to be a librarian than a farmer — ignoring that farmers
vastly outnumber male librarians.

The whole machinery of the lesson ahead is, in one sentence: *a disciplined
way of making the base rate impossible to ignore.*

::: {.callout-tip}
## The one habit to build now
Whenever you hear a claim of the form "the test/detector/witness is X%
accurate," train yourself to immediately ask: **"and how common is the thing
it's detecting?"** If you have that reflex, the rest of the lesson will feel
like filling in details.
:::

## Notation cheat sheet

| Symbol | Read as | Lesson role |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| $P(H)$ | probability of hypothesis H | **prior** — belief before evidence |
| $P(E \mid H)$ | probability of the evidence, if H is true | **likelihood** |
| $P(H \mid E)$ | probability of H, given the evidence | **posterior** — belief after evidence |
| $P(E)$ | overall probability of the evidence | normalizer (the "denominator") |

Ready? Head to the [lesson plan](index.qmd), or jump straight into the
[worked examples](bayes-in-action.qmd).
